blocks.scenario: Energistics is a mid-size energy company currently running a legacy ERP suite across four business divisions. The CIO has commissioned a new enterprise architecture engagement. The lead architect has completed Phase A — Architecture Vision, received sign-off on the Statement of Architecture Work, and has a confirmed scope and set of Architecture Principles. The stakeholders are aligned and the sponsor has formally approved the programme to proceed.
blocks.question: What should the architect do next?

options.text: Begin Phase B — Business Architecture. Develop the Baseline and Target Business Architectures, identify gaps, and produce Business Architecture components of the Architecture Roadmap.
Return to Phase A to refine and expand the Architecture Vision before proceeding, since the legacy ERP complexity may require a broader scope statement.
Jump to Phase E — Opportunities and Solutions to identify quick wins and assess migration feasibility early in the cycle.
Conduct a Preliminary Phase review to re-tailor the TOGAF framework for the energy sector before continuing.

blocks.rationale: Phase B — Business Architecture is the correct next step. The ADM is a sequential cycle: after Phase A sign-off, the architect moves to Phase B. The Statement of Architecture Work approval is the formal gate. Returning to Phase A (B) ignores that gate. Jumping to Phase E (C) violates the ADM sequence without a justified iteration trigger. Re-running Preliminary (D) is only appropriate before the first ADM cycle begins, not mid-engagement.
